text.title: cheap-guardrail-tripwire Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for cheap-guardrail-tripwire as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Guardrail agent declared with tools=[search, fetch, lookup_user] so it can "investigate" before deciding tripwire.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Now every legitimate request runs a multi-turn tool loop on the cheap model. Average pre-agent cost rises 5-20x; the only request type that stays cheap is the trivially-rejected one, which was already cheap with a single classification call.

failure-mode.description: Adding output_guardrails to a chat endpoint that streams tokens to the user without buffering.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Output guardrails see the final output, so the stream must be fully buffered before the guardrail runs and ANY of it can be sent. First-token latency goes from 200ms to multi-second, defeating the streaming UX. Open Agents SDK issue #495 documents this.

failure-mode.description: Using the same model for guardrail and main agent ("just to be safe gpt-5 will catch the jailbreaks too").
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The cost ratio collapses to 1.0 — the guardrail no longer saves anything; it doubles the cost of legitimate requests because they pay for both the guardrail call AND the main agent. Use a model at least 5-10x cheaper than the main agent.
